questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Termination of easement by necessity
questions.stem: A landowner divided a tract, conveying an interior parcel that had no access to a public road except across the retained parcel. An easement by necessity arose across the retained parcel. Ten years later, the owner of the interior parcel acquired an adjacent strip that provided practical and legally enforceable access to a public highway. The original easement agreement contained no express duration because the easement arose by operation of law.
questions.call: Does the easement by necessity continue?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because an easement by necessity ordinarily terminates when the necessity permanently ends.
choices.rationale: The later-acquired access removes the necessity that justified the implied easement.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because every easement is perpetual unless expressly released in writing.
choices.rationale: An easement by necessity is inherently tied to continuing necessity and can end without a written release.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because the easement became appurtenant when it was first used.
choices.rationale: Appurtenant status does not prevent termination when the foundational necessity ceases.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, but only if the servient owner pays for improvements made to the easement.
choices.rationale: Termination does not depend on reimbursement absent a separate equitable basis.

explanation.testedIssue: Termination of easement by necessity
explanation.governingRule: An easement by necessity arises from severance of commonly owned land when strict necessity exists. Because necessity is its basis, the easement ordinarily terminates when permanent alternative access eliminates the necessity.
explanation.application: The interior owner now has legally enforceable highway access through the acquired strip. The original necessity has ended, so the implied easement terminates.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating all easements as permanent interests unaffected by the reason for creation.
explanation.memoryAid: Necessity easement lasts only as long as necessity.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Landlord liability for undisclosed latent defect known at lease inception
questions.stem: A landlord leased a house to a tenant for one year. Before the lease began, the landlord knew that a concealed defect in the basement stairs caused one step to collapse under ordinary weight. The defect was not discoverable by a reasonable inspection, and the landlord did not disclose it. Two weeks after moving in, the tenant's guest used the stairs normally, the step collapsed, and the guest was injured.
questions.call: Is the landlord likely liable to the guest?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because a landlord who transfers possession is never liable for conditions on leased premises.
choices.rationale: Traditional exceptions include undisclosed latent defects known to the landlord at lease inception.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the landlord concealed a known latent defect existing at lease inception.
choices.rationale: The landlord owes liability to the tenant and others on the premises with consent for injuries caused by such concealed known defects.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the guest was not a party to the lease.
choices.rationale: The latent-defect exception protects persons on the premises with the tenant's consent, not only the tenant.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the landlord expressly promised to repair the stairs.
choices.rationale: A separate promise to repair is unnecessary when the landlord knowingly conceals a latent defect existing at transfer.

explanation.testedIssue: Landlord liability for undisclosed latent defect known at lease inception
explanation.governingRule: A landlord is liable for injuries caused by a latent defect existing at the start of the lease if the landlord knew or had reason to know of it and failed to disclose it. The duty can extend to the tenant and persons on the land with the tenant's consent.
explanation.application: The stair defect was concealed, known to the landlord, and undisclosed at possession. The injured guest falls within the protected class.
explanation.commonTrap: Applying caveat lessee without checking traditional landlord-liability exceptions.
explanation.memoryAid: Known hidden defect at move-in must be disclosed.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Shelter rule protecting transferee from bona fide purchaser
questions.stem: An owner conveyed land to a grantee, who did not record. The owner later conveyed the same land to a purchaser who paid value and lacked notice of the first conveyance. Under the jurisdiction's notice statute, the purchaser thereby obtained priority over the first grantee. Before either deed was recorded, the purchaser gave the land to her brother, who knew of the first conveyance. The first grantee then recorded and sued the brother for possession.
questions.call: Who should prevail?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The first grantee, because the brother had actual notice and did not pay value.
choices.rationale: The shelter rule allows the brother to receive the purchaser's protected title even though he would not independently qualify as a bona fide purchaser.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The first grantee, because the purchaser lost her protected status when she transferred the land before recording.
choices.rationale: Under a notice statute, the purchaser obtained priority upon taking for value without notice; recording was not a condition of that priority.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The brother, because the shelter rule gives him the purchaser's superior title.
choices.rationale: A transferee from a protected bona fide purchaser ordinarily succeeds to the transferor's priority.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The brother, but only if he records before the first grantee.
choices.rationale: The purchaser already had superior title under the notice statute, and the shelter rule does not require the brother to win a later recording race.

explanation.testedIssue: Shelter rule protecting transferee from bona fide purchaser
explanation.governingRule: Under the shelter rule, anyone who takes from a bona fide purchaser ordinarily receives the purchaser's protected status, even if the transferee had notice of the prior interest or did not pay value. This preserves the marketability of the bona fide purchaser's title.
explanation.application: The purchaser obtained priority when she took for value without notice. Her brother succeeds to that protected title under the shelter rule despite his own notice and donee status. The first grantee's later recording does not restore priority.
explanation.commonTrap: Requiring every later transferee independently to qualify as a bona fide purchaser.
explanation.memoryAid: Once title is sheltered, successors stay dry.
